contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: Expected O(n) hashing is not automatically preferable to O(n log n) sorting when guarantees, constants, locality, comparison cost, or collision assumptions change the end-to-end cost.

items.prompt: Why does expected O(n) hash construction not prove that hashing is always faster than O(n log n) comparison sorting?

options.text: Expected bounds, constants, key-operation costs, locality, memory, guarantees, and consumed output structure all matter.
Asymptotic notation never compares algorithms.
Every comparison sort is actually O(n).
Expected hash time is a deterministic worst-case guarantee.

feedback.reason: The headline asymptotic term is one component of an end-to-end cost model.
feedback.details: Hashing can be preferable, but the universal claim fails when assumptions or required downstream work change.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.big_o_wrong: Asymptotic analysis is useful but not sufficient by itself.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_linear: General comparison sorting is O(n log n) in the standard model.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_deterministic: Expected and worst-case bounds are different.

items.prompt: Under an adversarial collision model, what can happen to n insertions into a conventional hash table if each insertion may inspect O(n) colliding entries?

options.text: Total construction can degrade to O(n²).
Construction remains guaranteed O(n).
Construction becomes O(log n).
All insertions together are O(1).

feedback.reason: A sequence of n operations can cost O(n) each in a pathological collision regime.
feedback.details: A robust comparison distinguishes expected hashing from deterministic or adversarial guarantees.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_linear: Expected constant-time insertion is not a universal worst-case guarantee.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant: At least n inputs must be processed.

items.prompt: A dataset is already sorted and the task is to count equal runs once. Which cost comparison is most accurate?

options.text: A run scan is O(n) time and O(1) auxiliary space; rebuilding a hash table offers no asymptotic gain.
Sorting again is required and costs O(n log n).
A hash table processes the entire dataset in O(1).
Hash counting uses O(1) auxiliary space regardless of distinct keys.

feedback.reason: Existing order is preprocessing that should be credited to the sorting-based path.
feedback.details: The comparison must start from the actual input state, not assume both candidates begin from unsorted data.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_again: Known sorted order can be consumed directly.

items.prompt: Why can sorting have favorable practical constants despite an O(n log n) comparison count?

options.text: Contiguous scans and sorting routines can have strong cache locality and low allocation overhead.
Sorting performs no comparisons.
Hash tables use no metadata or indirection.
Hardware removes the logarithmic factor from the algorithm.

feedback.reason: Asymptotic notation hides constant factors and memory-access behavior.
feedback.details: Hashing may pay for hash computation, indirection, allocation, and less predictable access.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_no_memory: Buckets, load factors, and entries add overhead.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.log_disappears: Hardware affects constants, not the asymptotic operation structure.

items.prompt: Keys are long strings with expensive hashing, but comparisons usually stop after the first few characters. What should the analysis do?

options.text: Include actual hash and comparison costs instead of treating both as identical O(1) primitives.
Choose hashing solely because table operations are expected O(1).
String sorting is invalid.
Key representation never affects cost.

feedback.reason: The abstract operation model must match the key domain.
feedback.details: An expected constant number of expensive hashes can lose to more numerous but cheap prefix comparisons.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_expected: Expected probe count does not make hashing the key free.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_forbidden: Strings can have a legal total order.

items.prompt: A result must be emitted in ascending key order after frequency counting. Which cost is missing from the claim "hashing is O(n)"?

options.text: Ordering the distinct hash keys, typically adding O(k log k) comparisons for k distinct keys.
No additional work is needed because hash iteration is ascending.
The input no longer needs to be scanned.
Output order has no effect on runtime.

feedback.reason: A cost comparison must include downstream transformations required by the result.
feedback.details: Hash counting plus key sorting can still be good, but it is not simply O(n) when ordered output is required.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.rehash_zero: A plain hash map has no universal ascending-order guarantee.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_removed: Counts still require processing all values.

items.prompt: For very small n, which statement is most defensible?

options.text: Either strategy may win in practice; correctness comes first and constants can dominate the small input.
Hashing must be faster because O(n) beats O(n log n) for every concrete n.
Sorting must be faster for every small input.
Benchmark speed can override an incorrect result contract.

feedback.reason: Asymptotic classes describe growth, not exact crossover points.
feedback.details: Small-input choices can be guided by simplicity, locality, allocation, and existing order after legal strategies are identified.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_always: Asymptotic dominance does not guarantee every small-instance timing.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_always: No universal constant-factor claim is justified.

items.prompt: Which statement correctly compares guarantees?

options.text: Typical hash lookup may be expected O(1), while comparison sorting can provide deterministic O(n log n) worst-case bounds.
Expected and worst-case bounds are interchangeable.
Comparison sorting has no worst-case analysis.
Hashing has no meaningful worst case.

feedback.reason: The required guarantee level is part of the strategy contract.
feedback.details: Expected hashing can be appropriate, but deterministic latency or adversarial inputs may favor sorting.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_expected_only: Algorithms such as merge sort and heap sort have deterministic bounds.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_never_worst: Collision behavior creates a worst-case cost.

items.prompt: Which costs belong in an end-to-end hash-versus-sort comparison? Select all that apply.

options.text: Building the table or sorting the data.
Hashing, equality, and comparison costs for the actual key type.
Ordered output, grouping scans, and query workload after preprocessing.
Only the names O(n) and O(n log n).

feedback.reason: A fair model includes every operation required to satisfy the workload and output contract.
feedback.details: Isolated asymptotic labels can hide decisive costs.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.headline_only: The headline terms omit assumptions and later work.

items.prompt: Which assumptions are commonly behind expected linear hash construction? Select all that apply.

options.text: A compatible hash function distributes the observed keys adequately.
The table maintains a suitable load factor and resizing policy.
The analysis is expected or average under stated input/hash assumptions.
Keys must already be sorted.

feedback.reason: Expected O(n) construction is conditional on the table and input model.
feedback.details: Those conditions should be stated rather than converted into a universal rule.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ordered_keys: Hash construction does not require sorted input.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.expected_model: It is not automatically a deterministic guarantee.

items.prompt: Which conditions can make sorting competitive or preferable despite O(n log n) comparisons? Select all that apply.

options.text: The result already requires sorted keys or equal runs.
Contiguous access and low allocation overhead matter.
A deterministic worst-case guarantee is required.
Only expected arbitrary-order membership is needed and memory is plentiful.

feedback.reason: Sorting becomes stronger when its order is consumed or when hash assumptions are undesirable.
feedback.details: The final choice remains workload-specific.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.arbitrary_membership_only: This usually strengthens the hash case rather than sorting.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.ordered_output: Sorting work is reused by the result.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.worst_case: A chosen sort can provide it.

items.prompt: Under which workload assumptions does expected hashing retain a defensible time advantage? Select all that apply.

options.text: The workload is dominated by equality membership or key-to-value updates.
No deterministic ordered output or range traversal is required.
Keys have a cheap, well-distributed compatible hash.
The workload requires a deterministic collision-independent bound.

feedback.reason: Hashing is strongest when the required relation, key model, and guarantee assumptions align.
feedback.details: A deterministic adversarial requirement calls for additional protection or another strategy.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.adversarial_guarantee: This weakens an ordinary expected hash argument.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.equality_queries: Hash state directly serves these operations.

items.prompt: Arrange the process for comparing expected hash cost with sorting cost.

elements.text: List preprocessing, queries, updates, and required output.
State expected/worst-case hash assumptions and the sorting algorithm's guarantees.
Include hashing, equality, comparison, allocation, and traversal costs.
Compare the complete legal strategies under the same input and output contract.

feedback.reason: The comparison must use aligned assumptions and the whole workload.
feedback.details: Otherwise O(n) versus O(n log n) can describe different problems.

items.prompt: Arrange the analysis when ordered frequency output is required.

elements.text: Model hash counting plus sorting k distinct keys and emitting counts.
Model sorting n values plus one run scan.
Add auxiliary memory, mutation, and guarantee requirements for both paths.
Choose only after comparing the complete costs and contracts.

feedback.reason: Both strategies can be correct, but their cost decompositions differ.
feedback.details: The number of distinct keys k can materially change the hash-plus-key-sort path.

items.prompt: Arrange the review of an "expected O(n), therefore always faster" claim.

elements.text: Identify the probability or hashing assumptions behind the expected bound.
Check collisions, key cost, memory, output order, and constants.
Compare against the required worst-case or latency guarantee.
Replace the universal claim with a conditional workload-specific conclusion.

feedback.reason: A valid expected bound remains useful after its scope is stated correctly.
feedback.details: The repair is to qualify the claim, not to deny all value of hashing.

items.prompt: Count frequencies of n values with k distinct keys, then emit keys in ascending order. Select the expected hash-path time and auxiliary space.
constraints: After counting, the k keys are comparison-sorted.
The output itself is excluded from auxiliary space.

feedback.reason: Hash counting is expected linear, but ascending output adds sorting of the distinct keys.
feedback.details: The table stores one entry per distinct key.

items.prompt: Count frequencies by sorting n values in place with a deterministic O(n log n) comparison sort and then scanning equal runs. Select total time and auxiliary space.
constraints: The chosen in-place sort uses O(log n) stack space.
The run scan uses O(1) extra state.

feedback.reason: The linear grouping scan does not change the sorting-dominated time.
feedback.details: The stated sort uses logarithmic recursion or partition stack space.

items.prompt: Build a conventional hash table from n keys under a pathological collision pattern where the ith insertion may compare against O(i) existing entries. Select total build time and table space.

feedback.reason: Summing O(1 + 2 + ... + n) collision work yields quadratic time.
feedback.details: The table still stores only O(n) entries.

items.prompt: Input is already sorted. Count equal runs and emit one record per distinct key. Select time and auxiliary space excluding output.
constraints: Only current key and count are stored.

feedback.reason: Known sorted order eliminates both hash construction and sorting.
feedback.details: This is a key counterexample to comparing strategies from an incorrect common starting state.

items.prompt: A hash table doubles its capacity when a load threshold is crossed. Across n expected O(1) insertions, resizing copies all existing entries at geometric capacities. Select expected sequence time, worst cost of one resizing insertion, and table space.
constraints: Hash distribution assumptions support expected O(1) non-resizing insertions.
A resizing insertion may copy Θ(n) current entries.

feedback.reason: Geometric resizing copies each earlier entry only across logarithmically spaced capacity changes, so total copying is linear.
feedback.details: This distinguishes expected or amortized sequence cost from the worst cost of one operation and prevents treating every hash insertion as a deterministic O(1).

items.prompt: A comparison-based algorithm must put n arbitrary distinct keys into total sorted order. Select the comparison lower bound and the time of a matching optimal comparison sort.
constraints: Only pairwise key comparisons reveal order.
No bounded-key counting or radix assumption is available.

feedback.reason: There are n! possible key orders, so a comparison decision tree needs logarithmic depth in n!, which is Ω(n log n).
feedback.details: The n log n term is not merely avoidable overhead when the output contract requires a full arbitrary-key order.

items.prompt: Hashing one key costs H and comparing two keys costs C. Count n keys by hashing, or sort them by comparison and scan. Select symbolic dominant operation costs.
constraints: Hash construction performs expected O(n) hash/equality operations.
Comparison sorting performs O(n log n) key comparisons.

feedback.reason: Expected operation counts must be multiplied by the actual key-operation costs.
feedback.details: The symbolic model explains why a larger asymptotic count of very cheap comparisons can compete with expensive hashing.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: Expected O(n) hashing is not automatically preferable to O(n log n) sorting when guarantees, constants, locality, comparison cost, or collision assumptions change the end-to-end cost.
